title: Counting with wc
subtitle: Count lines, words, and characters

sections.instruction: You have a 100-line server log. But how do you know it's 100 lines without counting manually? The `wc` command counts for you.
sections.prompt: Type `wc -l server.log` to count the lines in the log file.

onSuccess.message: 100 lines! `wc` stands for 'word count', and `-l` tells it to count lines instead. Quick and precise.

sections.instruction: How many errors are in the log? Combine `grep` with `wc` using a pipe.
sections.prompt: Type `grep "ERROR" server.log | wc -l` to count just the error lines.

onSuccess.message: 10 errors. `grep` found all the ERROR lines, then `wc -l` counted them. You'll use this pattern constantly — filter then count.

sections.instruction: Count the warnings too. Same pattern: grep to filter, pipe to wc to count.
sections.prompt: Type `grep "WARNING" server.log | wc -l` to count the warning lines.

onSuccess.message: 20 warnings. You just built a mini analytics pipeline — search then count. In real ops work, this is how you quickly assess the health of a system.

completionMessage: Combining commands is the real power of the terminal. `grep` finds, `wc` counts, `head` and `tail` trim, and `|` connects them all. Each tool does one thing well, and together they can answer almost any question about your data.
